title: Farmhouse Bed with Arch
summary: A planked farmhouse bed on 4x4 posts, topped with a jigsaw-cut arched header that lifts it above the standard flat-panel bed. Shown in queen.
description:
This is a paneled farmhouse bed — 1x6 boards nailed to 1x4 trim, framed by chunky 4x4 posts and 2x6 caps — with one detail that transforms it: an arched top on the headboard. The arch is a single 1x10 with a gentle curve cut out of it, and it's the difference between 'a bed I built from lumber' and 'a bed that looks designed.'

The arch itself is easy to cut and easy to get subtly wrong. The plan's proportions are worth following: the curve begins 5-1/2" in from each end and rises to 6" at the center. Draw it with a flexible batten or a string-and-pencil sweep rather than freehand, cut just outside the line with a jigsaw, and fair the curve with a sander — a lumpy arch reads as a mistake, a smooth one reads as a feature. This same technique converts any of the flat-panel farmhouse beds to an arched version, so the header numbers here are the transferable part.

The posts are the fussy structural moment: you mark them 5/8" in on each end, center the panel on those marks, predrill halfway through the post, and drive 4-1/2" screws into the panel where it's three boards thick. Hitting that thick sandwich is what makes the joint strong. The side rails and cleats are optional if you already own a metal bed frame; skip them and the bed drops onto your existing frame. This is written for queen — other sizes convert with the same method and different panel counts.

tools.note: Crosscutting the 4x4 posts and long 2x stock.
Cuts the arch — the signature detail of this bed.
Predrill the 4-1/2" post screws so the 4x4 doesn't split.
For nailing the 1x6 panel boards to the 1x4 trim.
Fairing the arch smooth is what makes it look intentional.

materials.name: 1x10, 8 ft (side rails)
materials.note: Optional — skip if using an existing metal bed frame.

cutList.note: Top and bottom trim for both panels.
Arch begins 5-1/2" from each end, rises 6" at center.
Optional — skip with an existing metal frame.

steps.title: Nail up the headboard and footboard panels
steps.body: Lay two 1x4 trim pieces down and line up the 1x6 panel boards across them. Nail through the panel boards into the back of the trim with glue and 1-1/4" brads. Do all 11 headboard boards onto their two trim pieces, then repeat with the shorter boards for the footboard. Check each panel for square as you go — a panel that racks now fights you at every later step.

steps.title: Confirm the panels are square
steps.body: Set both finished panels flat and check them for square across the diagonals. This is the last easy moment to fix a racked panel before it's buried under caps and posts — measure corner to corner both ways and adjust until the two diagonals match.

steps.title: Cut the arch and attach the header
steps.body:
Draw the arch on the 1x10: start the curve 5-1/2" in from each end and rise to 6" at the center. Use a flexible batten or a string sweep to get a fair curve, not freehand. Cut just outside the line with a jigsaw, then sand the curve smooth — a lumpy arch looks like an error, a faired one looks designed.

Flip the headboard panel over and attach the arched header across the top with 2" nails and glue, keeping all outside edges flush.

steps.title: Mount the panels to the 4x4 posts
steps.body: Mark each 4x4 post 5/8" in from the edge on the face that meets the panel, and center the panel on those marks. Predrill halfway through the post, then drive 4-1/2" screws through the post into the panel — aim for where the panel is three boards thick, so the screw bites into solid material. Do the headboard with the tall posts and the footboard with the short posts.

steps.title: Add the top caps
steps.body: Set the 2x6 top caps over the panel top rails on both the headboard and footboard, leaving a 1" overhang on all sides. Fasten with glue and screws. The cap frames the panel and covers the top edge of the boards.

steps.title: Attach cleats, end rail, and side rails (optional)
steps.body:
If you're building the full frame rather than using a metal one: attach the cleats to the inside faces with the bottom edge 9-1/2" from the bottom, predrilling and using four long screws each. Add the footboard end rail (J) inset to the cleats, three screws per end. Then position the footboard so the end rail sits flush with the panel and screw through it into the footboard.

Finally add the 1x10 side rails, keeping their bottom edge flush with the cleats, screwing from the inside of the cleat out into the rail with predrilled 2" screws down its length. Sand and finish the whole bed.

images.alt: Planked farmhouse bed with an arched headboard on chunky 4x4 posts
